quotes.verbatim_quote: The “clave” is a rhythmic “cell” that’s the root of most rhythmic patterns.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the foundational rhythmic concept from which all bossa nova guitar patterns in this chapter are derived.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The “clave” can be played as shown above or inverted, but once the
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the critical performance rule that clave direction must remain consistent throughout a piece, making reversal a stylistic error.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The “bossa nova” right hand patterns are based on the “clave” figure.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the direct structural link between the clave cell and the guitar right-hand patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Notice that on the guitar, the syncopation is played on the top voices of the
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the core right-hand technique split: fingers handle syncopated upper voices while the thumb anchors the beat on bass notes.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Like samba patterns, the bass line usually keeps switching between the root
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the bass-line construction rule and the preferred voicing direction (fifth below root), with the open 6th-string exception.

quotes.verbatim_quote: It’s also a good idea to practice away from the guitar, clapping hands on the
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes a body-independence drill that internalises the rhythmic split between bass and upper voices.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This variation is the basic pattern with a 16th note syncopation on the Ist
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines Variation #1 technically and establishes the conceptual equivalence of samba and bossa nova patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote: We now have an example of an extended pattern (2 measures long). You can
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the concept of a two-measure pattern unit and gives the practical performance context.

quotes.verbatim_quote: To play the five-note chords in this example, you must use right hand fingers 1,
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides explicit right-hand fingering instruction for dense voicings, extending the three-finger technique to five voices.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern is very useful at the singer’s pause or at the end of a
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a practical performance-context rule for when Variation #4 is idiomatically appropriate.

quotes.verbatim_quote: You can also syncopate by a 16th note.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces a 16th-note displacement sub-variant of Variation #4.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Notice that the harmony changes a 16th note
quotes.why_load_bearing: States a precise rule about the offset between harmonic rhythm and rhythmic syncopation, a subtle bossa nova detail.

quotes.verbatim_quote: It’s basically the “variation #3”, but using short notes, and playing only the
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies staccato articulation and registral reduction as a distinct sub-technique applied to an existing variation.
